summary: Stancil argues that in the AI era, software is drifting from craft toward culture: marketing, mindshare, and founder “vibes” can outweigh product quality when underlying tech is commoditized. Using Cluely as a case study, he suggests startups are increasingly like memecoins—vehicles to monetize viral fame—because software creation costs are collapsing and attention is scarce.
money_quotes: Taste and quality are dwarfed by marketing and mindshare.
Cluely isn’t a technology company; it’s a shock jock podcast that manufactures outrage.
It’s a meme-company, created to sell a meme while the meme is still popular.
Launching a new SaaS product isn’t that much harder than launching a memecoin.
In a world where foundation models improve 10x every 18 months, building infrastructure is like decorating a house you’re about to demolish.
key_insight: As AI commoditizes software creation, startups can function more like meme vehicles where attention and brand eclipse product craft.
